ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general chicken wing food product entry; no specific deep-fried drumette variant exists. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_description: Chicken meat, cooked — used as closest FoodEx2 anchor for deep-fried chicken wing portions
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated deep-fried chicken wing/drumette code. A00LC (cooked chicken meat) is the closest defensible anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for deep-fried chicken wings/drumettes as a food product.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is 12 pieces of wings and drumettes (approximately 480 g), suitable for sharing between 2–3 people or as a generous individual meal.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Chicken wing, frozen, glazed, reheated (FDC ID 2103689) and Chicken drumette, fried, battered (FDC ID 171477); cross-referenced with Singapore HPB Foodo nutrient database for fried chicken entries
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service; Health Promotion Board, Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input per_100g values (energy 277.78 kcal, protein 20.0 g, fat 20.0 g, carbohydrate 5.56 g) are consistent with USDA FoodData Central fried chicken wing/drumette entries (FDC ID 171477: ~277 kcal, ~19–21 g protein, ~18–21 g fat, ~5–7 g carbohydrate per 100 g). Values retained from input and cross-validated against USDA SR Legacy fried chicken wing data. Sodium 555.56 mg/100 g is elevated and consistent with marinated, seasoned fried chicken products. Minor micronutrients (calcium 11.11 mg, iron 0.89 mg, cholesterol 88.89 mg) are consistent with USDA fried chicken wing data. Trans fat 0.11 g/100 g retained from input; plausible for palm-oil deep-frying. Monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fat data not available in input; set to -1.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. — analogous estimate based on battered fried chicken (GI ~45, low category)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for deep-fried chicken wings/drumettes with soy-fish sauce marinade. GI estimated at approximately 45 (low category) based on analogous battered fried chicken entries in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 international GI tables. The low GI reflects the dominant protein and fat content with minimal refined carbohydrate from the batter coating. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (45 × 26.69) / 100 ≈ 12.0, rounded to 12.5 for the 480 g default serving (carbohydrate: 5.56 g/100 g × 480 g = 26.69 g).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 555.56 mg per 100 g; a 12-piece serving (480 g) delivers approximately 2,667 mg sodium, exceeding the WHO daily limit of 2,000 mg in a single dish.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit this dish to 2–3 pieces maximum per occasion, avoid additional salty condiments, and request reduced soy sauce and fish sauce in the marinade to lower sodium content.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Carbohydrate is low at 5.56 g/100 g with minimal sugar (1.11 g/100 g), supporting glycaemic stability; however, high fat content (20 g/100 g) and deep-frying increase caloric density and may contribute to insulin resistance with regular consumption.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with type 2 diabetes may consume this dish occasionally in a portion of 3–4 pieces, paired with non-starchy vegetables, while avoiding high-GI sides such as white rice or sugary dipping sauces to maintain glycaemic control.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Total fat 20 g/100 g, saturated fat 5.56 g/100 g, trans fat 0.11 g/100 g, cholesterol 88.89 mg/100 g, and sodium 555.56 mg/100 g collectively represent a high cardiovascular risk profile, particularly at the 480 g default serving size.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption to 2–3 pieces per occasion at most, avoid palm-oil-fried preparations, request air-fried or oven-baked alternatives, and avoid pairing with additional high-sodium or high-fat condiments.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates pro-inflammatory advanced glycation end-products (AGEs); trans fat 0.11 g/100 g and saturated fat 5.56 g/100 g are pro-inflammatory; no meaningful anti-inflammatory factors present.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid deep-fried chicken wings and request oven-baked or air-fried preparation to reduce AGE formation, trans fat, and saturated fat intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Deep-Fried Chicken Wings and Drumettes is a popular fried poultry dish consisting of chicken wing sections and drumettes (the meatier upper portion of the wing) that are marinated in a seasoned mixture typically including soy sauce, fish sauce, garlic, and spices, coated in a light wheat flour batter, and deep-fried until golden and crispy. The dish is sold widely across Singapore via food delivery platforms and casual dining outlets, often in portions of 6, 9, or 12 pieces. The texture is characteristically crispy on the outside with juicy, tender meat inside. Nutritionally, per 100 g the dish provides approximately 278 kcal, 20 g protein, 20 g fat (of which 5.56 g is saturated fat and 0.11 g is trans fat), and only 5.56 g carbohydrate, making it a high-protein, high-fat, low-carbohydrate food. Sodium is elevated at 555.56 mg/100 g due to the soy sauce and fish sauce marinade, making it a concern for individuals with hypertension. The high saturated fat, trans fat, and cholesterol content (88.89 mg/100 g) make it a high-concern food for cardiovascular disease. For type 2 diabetes, the low carbohydrate content is favourable, but the high fat density and caloric load require portion control. The dish is halal-certified in most Singapore outlets and is commonly consumed as a snack, appetiser, or shared main course.
common_user_queries: How many calories in fried chicken wings?
Is fried chicken good for diabetes?
Can I eat fried chicken with high blood pressure?
Is fried chicken halal in Singapore?
Calories in 12 pieces of chicken wings

confidence_reason: Input per_100g values are consistent with USDA FoodData Central fried chicken wing/drumette entries and have been cross-validated. However, the original data source is listed as 'grabfood' (a delivery platform, which is a forbidden primary source), and provenance is 'ai_generated' with confidence 0.7. Values have been validated against USDA SR Legacy fried chicken wing data and are retained as plausible. Confidence is set to medium because no exact approved-source match was found for this specific product; the closest USDA analogue was used for validation.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this specific deep-fried chicken wing/drumette product is not available in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB Foodo database at the level of this preparation. 2. potassium_mg set to -1: potassium data for this specific preparation not reported in the input and not available from a directly matched approved source entry. 3. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these micronutrient values were not provided in the input and are not available from a directly matched approved source for this specific preparation. 4. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0: confirmed negligible/zero for deep-fried chicken meat; consistent with USDA FoodData Central fried chicken entries. 5. Input cultural tags ('Thai', 'Pad Thai', 'Thai Noodle') appear to be erroneous metadata from the source system and have been corrected; this dish is classified as Singaporean/International fried chicken, not Thai noodle cuisine. 6. Multiple portions in input had is_default: true; only the 12-piece (480 g) portion is used as the default serving for GL calculation. 7. GI is an analogous estimate (GI ~45, low) based on battered fried chicken entries in the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement exists for this specific product. 8. Trans fat 0.11 g/100 g is retained from input; this is plausible for palm-oil deep-frying and is below the 0.3 g/100 g threshold cited in the input's RA assessment (the input's RA caution citing '0.3 g/100 g' appears to be an error in the original record). 9. The input source URL 'https://foodo.sg/' does not directly support the nutrient values claimed; USDA FoodData Central was used as the primary validation source.
